data.title: Architect of Hong Kong’s Octopus card retires with dreams of ‘tap-free’ technology
data.description:
Sammy Kam Chi-sum, the primary architect of Hong Kong’s contactless Octopus cards, is retiring after nearly 30 years with the company at the end of this month, before what he says is an inevitable transition to a technology that will not even require tapping for certain transactions.
Octopus’ chief operations officer began his journey in 1995 when he joined the MTR Corporation’s marketing team to help launch what would become the world’s first contactless system that revolutionised public transport and retail payments.
Now in his 60s, Kam said his vision was to create a “tap-free” commute environment by using ultra-wideband wireless technology with “centimetre-perfect” location tracking to enable passengers to move through entry and exit barriers without needing to tap a card on a sensor.
“I really hope that in the next two to three years, ultra-wideband can be fully realised. The system will sense your presence, deduct the fare [from your Octopus card] and open the gate for you automatically,” he said.
“Actually, the technology is already here. We are actively exploring it, but I will leave the rest for my successors to handle.”
Kam will be succeeded by Octopus’ head of transport business, Nora Tang Chui-shan, and the general manager of the company’s technical department Tracy Poon Suet-wing on New Year’s Day.
The MTR Corporation has a 64.02 per cent stake in Octopus, while the rest is owned by the Kowloon-Canton Railway Corporation, KMB, Citybus and Sun Ferry.
As he looked back on his time at Octopus, Kam did not shy away from some of the company’s less savoury moments.
In 2010, it sold the personal data of its nearly 2 million users to third-party entities for marketing, earning HK$44 million (US$5.7 million) – a scandal that Kam said he still recalled “vividly”.
Following initial denials and a subsequent public inquiry, Octopus’ CEO at the time, Prudence Chan Bik-wah, resigned under immense pressure.
Chastened, Octopus pledged to pay closer attention to corporate social responsibility by donating its data profits to charity the following year.

There were other missteps, Kam admits, but they arose from technical flaws and carelessness rather than corporate misgovernance. During its early phase, Octopus cards were deactivated after six months of inactivity. Once, when a bus passenger used an expired card, the reader beeped with the message, “Illegal usage”.
“The bus driver didn’t know how to handle it, so he called the company and was told to take the passenger to the police station. It was a very unfortunate incident. I still remember it clearly today, and I still feel I owe an apology to that passenger,” Kam said.
Also retiring after a 17-year tenure is sales and marketing director Rita Li Yuk-yi, who looked back on the high-stakes negotiations that led to the integration of Octopus into Samsung Pay in 2017 and Apple Pay in 2020.
“We thought it was very hard to talk to a global handset manufacturer because we were a local payment [system], not a global payment service like Mastercard or Visa,” she said.
“But I met a contact person and asked about lining up with Samsung. That was how we truly began a dialogue and even went to Samsung’s headquarters in [South] Korea to talk to them and pitch the whole thing.”
Li, who is in her 60s, also highlighted the intensity of working with Apple, which had very high standards.
“We learned a lot from them. Whether it was technical or commercial, [Apple] was very demanding, especially regarding loading speed and stability,” she said.
Kam and Li navigated the competitive landscape of the government’s consumption voucher scheme, which was launched during the Covid-19 pandemic in phases.
The company rolled out over HK$250 million in cash rewards and welcome offers to maintain its dominance over rival stored-value platforms.
“We brainstormed many strategies and had to think carefully about how to roll everything out, how to launch [offers] and when. Besides getting customers, we also had to figure out how to incentivise them to actually use [the vouchers],” Li said.
“It was actually a very challenging project, but I’m also very happy because the team managed to gain the most support from the public in using their consumption vouchers with us.”

As they approached retirement, both leaders said they looked towards “paying it forward”, with Li planning to dedicate her time to mentoring teenagers.
She will be succeeded by the company’s retail business and partnership general manager, Edwin Lai Ka-chung.
Kam said he wanted to contribute to the development of the city’s innovation and technology sector.
“I have experience and knowledge to help others do things that are useful to society or help people, it would be a way to give back,” he said.
“I’m very willing to participate in being an incubator or adviser to help local start-ups and promote Hong Kong’s innovation and technology development.”

data.title: With 1MDB verdict due, will Malaysia’s Najib have to ‘prepare for the worst’?
data.description:
Malaysia’s High Court is poised on Friday to deliver its verdict in the final criminal trial of jailed former prime minister Najib Razak, bringing to a close one of Southeast Asia’s most far-reaching corruption sagas.
The case centres on allegations that the disgraced 72-year-old misappropriated 2.3 billion ringgit (US$490 million) from the scandal-ridden Malaysian sovereign wealth fund 1Malaysia Development Berhad (1MDB). Najib is currently in Kajang Prison, over halfway through a sentence reduced to six years for a conviction in a separate case linked to a former 1MDB subsidiary.
Prosecutors allege the money was siphoned off through a complex network of banks, shell companies and intermediaries while Najib was both prime minister and finance chief.
The High Court in Kuala Lumpur is expected to rule on four counts of abuse of power and 21 charges of money laundering. If Najib is found guilty, he could face lengthy prison terms that could run into decades, depending on whether any sentences are ordered to be served consecutively.
The decision will be delivered at the Palace of Justice in Putrajaya and will be closely watched by a Malaysian public split over its opinion towards Najib, who is adored by his loyalists and loathed by critics for the corruption that billowed out under his administration.
It will be delivered by Justice Collin Lawrence Sequerah, who presided over the trial and has since been elevated to the Federal Court.

A case years in the making
The trial is the last unresolved criminal proceeding against Najib linked to 1MDB, the scandal that erupted in 2015 and ultimately contributed to the collapse of his government three years later.
The trial stretched over seven years, with prosecutors calling 50 witnesses across 235 days, while the defence presented 26 witnesses, including Najib himself.
Prosecutors sought to prove that Najib was not an unwitting bystander, misled by aides or financiers – as his defence claimed – but was, in fact, the central decision-maker at 1MDB who controlled its affairs and benefited from its funds. They alleged the money moved through Najib’s personal accounts at AmIslamic Bank in Kuala Lumpur between 2011 and 2014.
Najib’s defence team, led by senior lawyer Muhammad Shafee Abdullah, has consistently denied his client’s wrongdoing.
The defence argued that the funds were legitimate political donations from Saudi Arabia and that Najib was deceived by shadowy fugitive financier Low Taek Jho, better known as Jho Low, whom Shafee described as the “main architect” of the plundering scheme.
Low remains at large and is wanted by authorities in Malaysia, the United States and Singapore.

Global fallout
Malaysia’s taxpayers lost billions of dollars to the 1MDB scandal. But it has also triggered investigations and prosecutions across multiple jurisdictions, including the US, Luxembourg, Switzerland and Singapore, in what analysts say is one of the most egregious examples of kleptocracy in the modern era.
US prosecutors previously described the affair as one of the largest corruption cases ever pursued by the Department of Justice, alleging that more than US$4.5 billion was looted from the fund and pushed through banks and assets across the world.
Stolen money was traced to luxury real estate in New York and Los Angeles, a superyacht once owned by Low, and works by artists including Monet and Van Gogh.
Hollywood was also pulled into the scandal, with funds linked to 1MDB used to finance The Wolf of Wall Street, starring Leonardo DiCaprio, who later returned artworks and other gifts tied to the case after US authorities launched asset recovery proceedings.
Former Goldman Sachs banker Roger Ng Chong Hwa was convicted in the US in April 2022 over his role in helping raise funds for 1MDB.
He was later extradited to Malaysia in October 2023 to assist local investigations, following the return in July that year of former 1MDB lawyer and Jho Low associate Jasmine Loo Ai Swan after years on the run.
The Malaysian government in October said it had so far recovered about 20.73 billion ringgit through settlements with financial institutions linked to the scandal.
This includes US$3.9 billion from a settlement with Goldman Sachs, comprising US$2.5 billion in cash and a US$1.4 billion asset recovery guarantee, as well as 2.83 billion ringgit from AmBank Group and US$330 million from JPMorgan (Switzerland).

High stakes
Najib has been incarcerated at Kajang Prison since August 2022 after being convicted in a separate case involving SRC International, a former 1MDB subsidiary.
That conviction, which involved 42 million ringgit, initially carried a 12-year jail term that was later reduced to six years following partial royal clemency. He has been tirelessly pushing the courts to allow him to serve his time under house arrest rather than in jail.
But earlier this week, the High Court rejected Najib’s bid to serve the remainder of that sentence under house arrest, ruling that an alleged royal addendum permitting such an arrangement had no legal effect.
It remains unclear whether Najib would remain in prison immediately if convicted in the 1MDB case, though his lawyers have said they will seek a stay of execution pending appeal, potentially taking the case to the Court of Appeal and the Federal Court.
Political analyst Oh Ei Sun said the latest developments had reinforced expectations that Najib faced an uphill battle in court.
“Well, as with all indicted, he has to prepare for the worst,” Oh told This Week in Asia.

data.title: Maximalist cocktails are back as bartenders go beyond garish to create ‘something special’
data.description:
After years of minimalist, pared-back cocktails, maximalism is back.
Drinkers are looking for more than just a simple serve, and bartenders are dreaming up bold colour palettes, layered flavours, oversized garnishes, theatrical glassware and playful twists on classic drinks.
The trend harks back to the “really out there” drinks service of the ’90s, says Hannah Sharman-Cox, co-founder of The Pinnacle Guide, which rates cocktail bars around the world with a “pin” system.
“It’s a little bit like that era’s grown-up cousin has taken the reins – more decadent luxury rather than garish waste,” says Sharman-Cox.
“Even the simple martini is starting to get more elaborate garnishes – big pickles, colourful cornichons – and we’re here for it,” she adds.

Mason Park, the general manager at Alice Cheongdam, in Seoul, South Korea, agrees that today’s maximalism takes a more thoughtful approach.
In the past, it was about flashiness and “prop” garnishes. The focus now, he says, is on the flavour impact, as well as sight, touch and smell.
There is also thought given to environmental sustainability, according to Siobhan Payne, The Pinnacle Guide’s other co-founder.
“Garnishes are increasingly designed to be edible or reusable, so we’re seeing more elaborate partnerships between the bar and the kitchen to create something delicious that truly complements the cocktail,” she says.
For Park, creative, attention-grabbing drinks also fuel and sustain the art of mixology.
“We work with so many elaborate elements and get so many ‘wow’ reactions from customers when we serve them, we believe that this maximalism – making these fun cocktails – actually plays a role in sustaining the profession of bartending itself,” he says.

Especially at a time when people are more cautious with their money, the industry needs to level up to give them a reason to visit bars – “not just for the simple experience of enjoying the night, but also to get something completely different”, says Emanuele Pedrazzani, head bartender at Nightjar, an underground bar in London.
At Alice Cheongdam, which takes its inspiration from Lewis Carroll’s Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland, the signature cocktail is Foggy Fongo, a riff on the book’s famous depiction of a caterpillar smoking a pipe on top of a mushroom.
Served in a smoky, mushroom-shaped glass, the cocktail is built on bourbon, frankincense and palo santo (a Peruvian wood), with macadamia and hazelnut, as well as artichoke, sherry and the prized pine mushroom.
“We extract the aroma from pine mushrooms using glycerine, and then we infuse that mushroom fragrance into the cocktail as smoke,” Park says.
The cocktail also features a truffle cookie, which can be accessed by opening the 3D printed mushroom-shaped cap.
“You can think of it as a cocktail that offers an experience where you eat and drink together,” Park says.
On the other side of the world, Nightjar has the elaborately presented Beyond the Sea cocktail, served in a giant shell. It is loosely inspired by the Salty Dog (gin or vodka and grapefruit juice with a salty rim), with a combination of gin, fino sherry with kombu seaweed, shiso leaves, limoncello, absinthe and grapefruit, finished off with a briny foam to evoke sea spray, and an olive.
It is an immersive experience, and it is hard to know where to start.
“From one of the narrow ends!” Pedrazzani quips. He says he loves to make customers laugh – “to create some sort of connection” – and he jokes that in this case, sometimes they do not listen and the drink ends up all over them.
At the Bon Vivants craft cocktail bar in Nassau, the Bahamas, experience is everything.
If you order the Mama Maggie, the fruity mix of coconut rum, passion fruit liqueur, hibiscus, mango, lime and orange juice will be delivered to you in a photogenic porcelain pig topped with colourful fresh flowers.

“When it comes to these types of drinks, customers want something they can post,” says Niko Imbert, Bon Vivants’ senior vice-president of hospitality.
They also want to see you put care and effort into the product, he adds.
“It’s just like a culinary experience in my opinion. If I see someone taking their time to create something special for me, I’m very thankful for it.”
To up your maximalist cocktail game at home, Imbert says, consider using vintage and unusual glassware, bar tools, garnishing, glitter or even a cocktail smoker.
“Just don’t go overboard, right?” says Jose Maria Donde, beverage manager and head mixologist at The Bedford Stone Street in New York.
He creates a theatrical take on the Paloma, the Rey Bucanero (Spanish for “King Buccaneer”). Alongside the traditional tequila and grapefruit, it contains rum, elderflower, cacao liqueur and yuzu, and is garnished with shiso leaf “waves” and an orchid flower.
At home, Donde suggests, start with something “that’s going to make you feel good and looks nice”, and add to it later as needed.

data.title: China sharpens sales pitch for overseas investment with new list of sought-after sectors
data.description:
China is taking steps to reshape the direction of its foreign investment, unveiling a revised catalogue of encouraged industries that steers overseas capital towards the advanced and technologically complex sectors Beijing’s policymakers view as future growth drivers.
The revised catalogue, which takes effect in February 2026, covers 1,679 industries – 205 more than the current version, which was released in 2022.
In a statement on Wednesday, the Ministry of Commerce said the changes are aimed at “strengthening China’s appeal to foreign investors, improving support services for foreign-funded firms and creating a more transparent, stable and predictable business environment”.
The revised catalogue widens the scope of incentives for overseas investment in advanced manufacturing, adding new fields to its coverage umbrella. Among others, these include nucleic acid drugs, high-end medical equipment, intelligent testing instruments, smart energy systems and deep-sea robots.
Yuan Qian, a lead researcher at the Chinese Academy of Macroeconomic Research, was quoted by state broadcaster CCTV as saying the catalogue is a core policy tool for promoting foreign investment and optimising the country’s industrial mix.
He said the catalogue helps China “better counter an adverse external environment by offsetting external uncertainty with the certainty of its own development, while providing global investors with clear, stable and transparent investment expectations”.
Greater emphasis has also been placed on modern services, with high-end shipping, virtual power plants, pet healthcare, sports tourism, camping, homestays, property services, internet-enabled healthcare and other sectors added to the catalogue.


At the same time, the revision sets up avenues for more foreign inflows in certain regions, adding sectors like cruise tourism, ice-and-snow equipment, marine ecological restoration, computing infrastructure and renewable energy projects. These fields, and others new to the catalogue, are concentrated in central China, as well as the country's west and northeast.
The revised catalogue is being published amid a campaign by Beijing to coax back foreign investment, as the massive inflows that once aided China's growth and development have slowed considerably in recent years.
In 2024, the world’s second-largest economy attracted 826.2 billion yuan (US$116.2 billion) of foreign investment, down 27.1 per cent year on year and dropping 32.9 per cent from the peak of 1.23 trillion yuan recorded in 2022.
The slide has continued this year. China’s total foreign investment over the first 11 months of 2025 reached 693.2 billion yuan, down 7.5 per cent compared with the same period last year.
To implement the new edition of the catalogue, the commerce ministry said it will step up policy communication, refine supporting measures and strengthen service guarantees to ensure foreign firms fully benefit from the revised framework.
In its statement, the ministry said it would “proactively coordinate and resolve difficulties and issues faced by foreign-invested enterprises during policy implementation.”
Foreign-invested projects listed in the catalogue are eligible for major policy incentives, the ministry said, including tariff exemptions on equipment imported specifically for project use, preferential treatment in land supply and discounted land prices for intensive industrial projects.
They will also have access to a reduced 15 per cent corporate income tax rate in the country's western regions, as well as the southern island province of Hainan; additionally, tax credits will be available for overseas investors who reinvest distributed profits into encouraged sectors.

data.title: ‘I have more to give’: Hong Kong Christmas lighting designer still at it after 40 years
data.description:
On a weekday night last week, passengers on an open-top double-decker bus jostled for a position to take selfies below a suspended lighting display at Mody Lane in Tsim Sha Tsui East, where Hong Kong residents have viewed the city’s dazzling Christmas lights for decades.
With 175 illuminated stars spread across 25 rows of dangling lights, the fixture is the work of veteran lighting designer Terence Wong Kim-shan, who has designed and installed Christmas lights for many of the city’s skyscrapers for over 40 years.
The Mody Lane installation is one of his favourite lighting displays this year.
“Every part of this display took a lot of time and effort to pull off,” he said. “From when we started the manufacturing work to putting it together on site, the process took about four months, so you can say we put a lot of thought into this.”
Christmas for the 67-year-old begins months in advance, around May, when his company starts designing the lights that will adorn Hong Kong’s skyscrapers.
“In August, we have the contracts signed for the Christmas lights. In September, we start the manufacturing process for the components. In October, we install the lights. In November, it is almost time for us to switch on the lights,” said Wong, the managing director of his company, Shun Sze Lighting Advertising Design Company.
“We follow the same schedule every year, that’s how I’ve spent the past few decades.”
Wong began his career repairing appliances but was soon brought into the stage lighting industry by a friend. In the early 1980s, a Tsim Sha Tsui East property developer asked if he could decorate the buildings to bring people into the then newly developed district.
Wong said that as the years have gone by, his lighting displays have become more complex and advanced, both in terms of the patterns and the lights used to create them.
“The lights in the past used to be more basic, featuring patterns such as stars, English words like ‘Season’s Greetings’ or a big snowflake. They would be more simple and we would build them using light bulbs,” he said.
“As Christmas lights evolved, [the designs] became much more complex. I have made a lot of different designs like astronauts in space, astronauts underwater and Santa Claus as the jade emperor in the celestial palace.”
With lights transitioning to LED and involving computer programs, Wong has had to keep up with the latest technological developments.
“I’m no longer someone who does electrical engineering; I’m now someone who has to know computers, programming and lights … I have adapted with the lights.”
Through the decades of putting up festive lights on the city’s skyscrapers, Wong’s most memorable Christmas installation was one he created for the Hong Kong Tourism Board in 2003 involving a Christmas tree set on top of a fountain in Central.

The display took roughly 14 days to put together and was extremely challenging to set up, Wong recalled.
“It was glorious, but it was also really challenging, as the Christmas tree was so heavy and had to sit in the middle of a fountain,” he said. “The fountain also could not leak; if it did, people could get electrocuted and we could be sued.”
Knowing that thousands of people see his work in public spaces, Wong often observes the public’s reaction and is not above tweaking his designs based on their feedback.
“You listen to them talking about it, you watch them taking photos and you have an idea of the public opinion on whether your lights have done well or not,” he said.
Despite working in the sector for more than four decades, Wong said he had no plans of slowing down or retiring as he was “too busy” to consider it.
“There are just so many things that are still waiting for me to do, maybe I’ll consider it when my age becomes an issue,” he said.
“But I still feel like I have more to give, so I’ll keep going and I will figure it out when the time comes.”

data.title: Trip.com suspends tourism partnership with Cambodia amid border clashes, cyber concerns
data.description:
Trip.com Group, the biggest online travel agency in China, said on Thursday that its partnership with Cambodia’s tourism authority was suspended, in a move to address the Chinese public’s heightened sensitivity over safety in the Southeast Asian country.
The company, which operates the Ctrip and Qunar sites in mainland China, said the collaboration – originally scheduled to run from September 2025 to March 2026 – “had been suspended due to recent safety alerts issued by the Chinese Embassy in Cambodia”.
“The partnership never actually launched,” the company said after the initiative, announced earlier this month, sparked an online backlash. As tensions between Cambodia and Thailand have escalated, some Chinese travellers expressed fears of being targeted by cross-border scam syndicates.

Since the start of December, the Chinese Embassy has issued five warnings, four regarding border clashes and one about scams. In recent years, some Chinese have been lured abroad with the promise of high-paying jobs, especially to Southeast Asia, only to end up working in scam factories. The embassy said that 46 Chinese nationals had successfully been rescued from digital scam centres in Cambodia since late November.
In the Thursday statement, Trip.com also moved to quell fears regarding data security, stating that its deal with Cambodia “does not involve any data cooperation and under no circumstances does it entail the disclosure of user privacy information”. Its Cambodia deal had prompted some users to threaten to uninstall the app from their phones as a boycott.
Trip.com added that it “undergoes annual network data security risk assessments by relevant authorities, having successfully completed the 2025 assessment in October”.
The suspension comes as Cambodia is trying to attract more Chinese tourists, with plans to trial visa-free visits by Chinese citizens from June 15 to October 15, 2026. In the first 10 months of 2025, Cambodia received around 1 million Chinese tourists, accounting for more than 20 per cent of the total of 4.8 million international visitors, according to the country’s tourism minister Huot Hak.
The Cambodia Tourism Board earlier this month insisted that the country “remains safe and fully open” and its “primary destinations”, including Siem Reap and Phnom Penh, operate “without interruption”.

data.title: Japan still popular with Hong Kong travellers over Christmas, Harbin gaining appeal
data.description:
More Hongkongers are travelling over the Christmas holiday period compared with last year, with traditional destinations in Japan remaining popular despite geopolitical tensions, and mainland Chinese cities such as Harbin and Yunnan gaining appeal.
Some industry insiders said Japan remained popular among Hong Kong travellers despite recent news of earthquakes and a geopolitical row between Beijing and Tokyo, which earlier saw mainland visitors cancelling their trips in droves.
Relations between Japan and China cooled significantly following remarks in early November by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, who said an attack on Taiwan by the People’s Liberation Army could constitute a “survival-threatening situation” and allow Tokyo to engage in military action.
In response, Beijing issued official travel advisories urging citizens to avoid going to Japan due to security concerns, with Chinese airlines cancelling over 40 per cent of flights between the two countries in December.
Takaichi sought to ease tensions earlier this month by saying Tokyo’s position remained “unchanged” from a 1972 joint communique with China.
Outbound travel started on Christmas Eve on Wednesday, when the number of Hong Kong residents departing the airport jumped 22 per cent to 65,133 from the same day a year ago, according to the Immigration Department.
The two consecutive public holidays on Christmas Day on Thursday and Boxing Day on Friday form an extended four-day weekend.
Moon Yau Moon-yee, assistant general manager at Sunflower Travel Services and vice-chairman of the Tourism Industry Employees’ General Union, explained that, regardless of geopolitical concerns, the unique winter appeal of Japan continued to drive demand among Hongkongers.
“Hokkaido’s snow scenery, hot springs and skiing activities have a unique attraction for Hong Kong tourists, ensuring that Japan tour groups remain in short supply,” he said.
Travel platform Trip.com’s data further highlighted that travellers were diversifying their itineraries within the country, with bookings for niche destinations such as Hiroshima recording triple-digit year-on-year growth, a company spokeswoman told the Post, without specifying what the figures were.
Trip.com data also showed Hongkongers were averaging three overseas trips per year, higher than the global average of 2.4 trips, with Christmas and Easter the annual peak periods for departures.
The mainland also emerged as a major competitor for travel among Hong Kong residents, owing to the difference in the holiday calendar across the border.
“Since the mainland does not have Christmas holidays, the flow of people does not increase significantly, which has become a major reason for attracting Hong Kong people to visit during this period,” Yau said.
Destinations in northeast China were especially popular this year, with places such as Harbin particularly favoured, as the city hosted its " title="" target="_self">largest-scale Ice and Snow World festival to date.
Other regions that recorded significant growth included Yunnan, where the number of visitors tripled compared with last year, as well as Guizhou and Shandong.
For those planning shorter “flash trips” over the four-day weekend starting December 25, South Korea and Southeast Asia were the preferred choices.
Yau said the favourable exchange rate of the Korean won had boosted the appeal of cities such as Seoul and Busan.
Experience-driven travel was also becoming a defining characteristic of the Hong Kong travellers’ market.
Trip.com reported that 2026 would see a continuation of concert tourism, with spikes in bookings linked to major performance tours such as those by K-pop girl group Blackpink and solo artist G-Dragon.

This trend was complemented by a high demand for international theme parks and immersive exhibitions.
Despite the high volume of travel, concerns regarding safety and volatility remained significant among Hong Kong residents.
According to the 2025 Allianz Partners Travel Index, 53 per cent of travellers expressed anxiety about personal safety, while 52 per cent were concerned about extreme weather.
Such anxieties led to a more cautious approach to trip planning, with 90 per cent of respondents indicating that “purchasing insurance is essential for peace of mind and protection against unforeseen events”.
Fanny Yeung Shuk-fun, executive director of the Travel Industry Council, said on Wednesday that South Korea had emerged as a more popular destination than Japan this Christmas.
“The main reason is that Japan has experienced several incidents in the past one or two months, including bear attacks and earthquakes, which have caused its popularity to drop slightly,” she said.

data.title: Hong Kong to broaden crypto rules with new licences for dealers and custodians
data.description:
Hong Kong will broaden its crypto rule book next year, with regulators preparing new licensing regimes for virtual asset dealers and custodians, as the city pushes to deepen its digital asset market even as Beijing tightens its scrutiny on crypto activities on the mainland.
Regulators said on Wednesday they would finalise legislative proposals for two new licensing frameworks – one for providers of virtual asset dealing services, previously referred to as over-the-counter (OTC) trading services, and another for custodian services – with the aim of introducing a bill into the Legislative Council in 2026.
Since announcing its ambition in late 2022 to become a leading digital-asset hub, Hong Kong has rolled out licensing regimes for crypto exchanges and stablecoin issuers and encouraged tokenisation activities, but such initiatives have faced hurdles such as high compliance costs and heightened scrutiny.
In a government statement, SFC chief executive officer Julia Leung called the latest plans “significant progress” that would keep Hong Kong “at the global forefront” of digital asset market developments, and said that the city would maintain “an unwavering commitment to responsible innovation”.

The latest measures reflect Hong Kong’s push to deepen its digital asset market even as the sector faces growing headwinds.
A regulatory regime for virtual asset custodian services was “particularly important” because properly regulated custodians were “key for greater retail and institutional adoption of crypto” as well as Hong Kong’s global digital asset hub status, said Andrew Fei, a partner at King & Wood Mallesons in Hong Kong.
Other major jurisdictions such as the US had already been “paving the way for digital asset custodians to be regulated in a more sensible manner”, Fei noted.
Hong Kong started formulating detailed plans to regulate OTC crypto trading services last year after one of the largest financial frauds in Hong Kong’s history, which involved the allegedly fraudulent crypto exchange JPEX that resulted in roughly HK$1.6 billion (US$225 million) in losses.
OTC services facilitate direct and private large-volume cryptocurrency transactions between two parties. Physical OTC shops were found to be one of the “main avenues for channelling retail investors’ funds” into fraudulent schemes in the JPEX case, regulators said at the time.

data.title: China sharpens criticism of Japan’s ‘neo-militarism’, signals readiness to respond
data.description:
Chinese state media have escalated criticism of what they characterise as Japan’s growing “neo-militarism”, warning that Beijing has sufficient policy tools in reserve to respond should Tokyo endanger China’s core interests.
People’s Daily, a Communist Party mouthpiece, said that “neo-militarism” – the resurgence of Japanese militarism after the end of the second world war – had dealt a serious blow to bilateral relations and undermined regional peace and stability.
“Post-war Japan has failed to carry out a thorough reckoning with militarism … This ‘neo-militarism’, cloaked in the guise of a ‘peaceful nation’, is gradually becoming a real danger to Asia and the world at large,” the commentary stated on Thursday.
The remarks coincide with heightened tensions in Sino-Japanese ties, as Beijing has continued to accuse Tokyo of taking steps to expand its military footprint.


Japan’s Official Security Assistance budget, which provides security equipment aid to like-minded countries, is set to reach a record 18.1 billion yen (US$116 million) under the foreign ministry’s budget proposal for financial year 2026, released on Tuesday – more than double the amount allocated in the previous financial year.
Last week, an unnamed senior Japanese security official in the prime minister’s office reportedly told local media off the record that Tokyo should possess nuclear weapons to deter potential aggressors.
The comments sparked backlash both domestically and abroad, prompting the Japanese government to later reaffirm its decades-old pledge never to possess nuclear arms.
On Wednesday, China’s foreign ministry said the official’s remarks were not an isolated case but part of a broader effort by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi and right-wing forces in Japan to test international red lines.
Foreign ministry spokesman Lin Jian said the comments exposed Tokyo’s “long-standing erroneous positions and continued push for ‘re-militarisation’, sending an extremely dangerous signal”.
In the two months since Takaichi took office, Japan has accelerated military expansion, bringing forward by two years its target of defence spending at 2 per cent of GDP, Lin said.
He added that Japan had also eased arms export restrictions, advanced revisions to key security documents and floated changes to the Three Non-Nuclear Principles in place since the 1960s, including hints at introducing nuclear-powered submarines.
Yuyuan Tantian, a social media account affiliated with Chinese state broadcaster CCTV, said in an article on Thursday that Beijing’s recent countermeasures such as sanctions and official statements showed its determination to “decisively block any possibility of a resurgence of Japanese militarism”.

Huo Zhengxin, a law professor at the China University of Political Science and Law, told Yuyuan Tantian that countermeasures targeting Japan were now at a stage of “preparation and observation” and that their direction was clear.
China had a range of policy tools in reserve, Huo said, and warned that if Beijing’s core interests were affected, the response would extend beyond diplomatic actions.
Bilateral relations have nosedived since Takaichi last month suggested an attack by Beijing on Taiwan could be seen as a “survival-threatening situation” for Japan that could trigger a military response.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary. Most countries, including the US and Japan, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
According to the People’s Daily commentary, Japan has made dangerous departures from its post-war framework across multiple domains.
It said Japan’s defence policy had rapidly shifted from an exclusively defensive posture towards a more autonomous, offensive-oriented approach, with the Self-Defence Forces now possessing “pre-emptive strike” capabilities.
It also blasted Japan’s “neo-militarism” as contradictory, arguing that the country “presents itself as a guardian of regional security while repeatedly provoking disputes”.
The commentary accused Tokyo of deliberately fanning tensions in recent years over issues such as the Taiwan Strait and the South China Sea.
Furthermore, it said that by strengthening alliances and quasi-allied ties with countries including the US, Britain and Australia, it had “intentionally fuelled bloc-based confrontation in the region”.
“Takaichi has shown militarist tendencies not only in her stance on the use of force regarding Taiwan, but also in promoting historical revisionism in education,” the article added.

data.title: Ex-China table tennis player says clubs hold key to return of Wang Chuqin, Sun Yingsha
data.description:
Former China table tennis player Ma Te said he believed that Wang Chuqin’s and Sun Yingsha’s participation at the Chinese Table Tennis Super League Finals would be decided by their respective clubs.
Ma’s comments, which were shared during a livestream on his Douyin account on Wednesday, come after Wang and Sun withdrew from the WTT Finals in Hong Kong because of injury.
Discussing whether Wang would play at the Super League Finals, Ma said this depended on what Wang’s club decided, adding that players would not be fined if they did not play.
Sun’s Shenzhen University team and Wang’s Shandong Weiqiao team have both trained at the venue in Nanjing, but fans have not seen either Wang or Sun on court, leading to ongoing discussion about whether they will appear.
“Both of them are feeling somewhat better, but the degree of recovery depends on their different injuries,” Ma said. “A week is definitely too short for a full recovery.”
Wang trained at a sports centre in Beijing on Monday for the first time since withdrawing from the WTT Finals, but it is believed that he will be only a spectator at the Super League Finals.

Ma said players had been focusing on recovery and adjustments after the WTT Finals, “while a week may not seem long, for core players who have faced a packed schedule, it is already a relatively adequate period for rest”.
Ma responded to fans’ concerns about the lack of updates about the players, saying: “This is actually a good thing.
“It means they are resting well and adjusting their treatment as needed. Everyone should give athletes time to recover and wait for them to return to competition when they are ready.”
Wang withdrew from his semi-final and Sun forfeited her semi-final through injury at the WTT Finals.
